book_claims.mechanism: High EPA/DHA (omega-3) content; reduces cardiovascular risk and inflammation; essential for cell membrane function
book_claims.text: Salmon is recommended as a high omega-3, omega-6, and vitamin B12 fish for the Longevity Diet

book_claims.mechanism: Rich in omega-3 fatty acids (EPA/DHA), vitamin D, vitamin B12, and protein; supports cardiovascular and brain health
book_claims.text: Wild-caught salmon is a top source of vitamin B12 (2.3–4 mcg per 75 g cooked, 38–67% DV), vitamin D (44–447 IU per 3 oz cooked, 11–112% DV), omega-3 (1.08–1.38 g EPA/DHA per 75 g), vitamin A (59 mcg RAE), and magnesium (26 mg per 3 oz); also a calcium source when canned with bones

supplementary_research.finding: Meta-analysis (PMID 17374686) found regular fatty fish consumption reduces coronary heart disease mortality by ~36%; EPA/DHA in salmon is a primary driver
Salmon omega-3s (EPA/DHA) reduce systemic inflammation markers including IL-6 and TNF-alpha (PMID 26263244), supporting longevity pathways
Salmon is one of the richest dietary sources of EPA and DHA; regular consumption linked to reduced triglycerides, improved HDL, and lower all-cause mortality in large cohort studies

nutrient_highlights.bioavailability_notes: Highly bioavailable marine omega-3s; superior to plant ALA sources
One of the richest food sources; fat in fish enhances absorption
Animal-sourced B12 is highly bioavailable without conversion
Carotenoid antioxidant; fat-soluble, enhanced by dietary fat co-ingestion

synergies.note: Olive oil polyphenols and salmon omega-3s together reduce cardiovascular inflammation through complementary pathways
Salmon is among the richest natural food sources of vitamin D, synergizing with its own calcium-sparing effect
Both low-mercury oily fish; rotating between them diversifies omega-3 and mineral intake
